description: Declarative catalog for the config module's permissions (C), privacy (D), and context (E) checks. Each entry carries: id, dimension, severity, title, detect (human-readable detection hint / provenance template), why (plain-English), fix (what to change, incl. exact snippet where relevant), and optional caveatContextual (the §4 plain+contextual caveat the audit skill should branch on whether a real exposure was found). The scanner implements the detection logic per id; this file is the single source of truth for the catalog text so checks are data, not hardcoded strings. {file} and {value} are interpolated by the scanner.

checks.title: Permission prompts are fully bypassed
checks.detect: settings `defaultMode` is "bypassPermissions" in {file}
checks.why: bypassPermissions skips every approval prompt — even for writes to .git and .claude. Outside an isolated container or VM, anything Claude or a script it runs decides to do happens with no checkpoint.
checks.fix: Set "defaultMode": "default" in settings. To lock the bypass off entirely, add { "permissions": { "disableBypassPermissionsMode": "disable" } }. Only use bypass in an isolated environment.
checks.caveatContextual: Only safe inside a throwaway container or VM. On your real machine this removes the last line of defense.

checks.title: Auto-approve mode is on
checks.detect: settings `defaultMode` is "auto" in {file}
checks.why: auto mode (a research preview) auto-approves actions without asking. Combined with broad Bash or read access, the blast radius is large.
checks.fix: Reconsider auto mode; set "defaultMode": "default". To lock it out add { "permissions": { "disableAutoMode": "disable" } }.

checks.title: Edits are auto-accepted by default
checks.detect: settings `defaultMode` is "acceptEdits" persisted as the default in {file}
checks.why: acceptEdits auto-accepts file edits — including rm/mv/cp inside the working directory — without a per-edit prompt. Fine for a focused session, risky as a permanent default.
checks.fix: Remove "defaultMode": "acceptEdits" from persisted settings and enable accept-edits per session only when you want it.

checks.title: Bash is allowed with no restriction
checks.detect: permissions.allow contains a bare `Bash` or `Bash(*)` rule in {file}
checks.why: A bare Bash allow drops the whole-tool approval prompt, so Claude can run any shell command without asking. That is effectively unrestricted command execution.
checks.fix: Remove the bare `Bash` / `Bash(*)` allow. Rely on per-command approval, or add specific allow rules like `Bash(npm test)`, `Bash(git status)`.

checks.title: Network commands (curl/wget) are pre-approved
checks.detect: permissions.allow contains `Bash(curl ...)` or `Bash(wget ...)` in {file}
checks.why: curl and wget are deliberately NOT auto-approved by Claude Code because they are the main way data leaves your machine. Allowing them turns any reachable secret into an exfiltration risk, and any web content into a prompt-injection vector.
checks.fix: Move curl/wget to permissions.deny. For fetching docs, use a WebFetch(domain:...) allowlist instead.
checks.caveatContextual: This is the channel a reachable secret would actually leave through — it matters most when the scan also found exposed secrets.

checks.title: A rule pins part of a sensitive command to limit it
checks.detect: permissions.allow constrains an argument on a network or destructive command, e.g. `Bash(curl http://x/ *)` or `Bash(rm /tmp/ *)` in {file}. Plain convenience allows like `Bash(git fetch *)` are NOT flagged.
checks.why: Pinning a web address or path inside an allow rule looks like it limits the command, but those patterns slip past easily (a different protocol, a redirect, a shell variable). If you're relying on this as a safety wall, it won't hold. If it's just a convenience, it's fine — just don't treat it as a lock.
checks.fix: If you only meant it as a convenience, nothing to do. To actually restrict, use a deny rule, or limit web addresses with a WebFetch(domain: …) rule instead.

checks.title: An indirect command runner is pre-approved
checks.detect: permissions.allow contains a runner like `Bash(npx ...)`, `Bash(docker exec ...)`, `Bash(devbox run ...)`, `Bash(uvx ...)` in {file}
checks.why: These commands can run arbitrary inner commands, so allowing them is effectively the same as allowing all of Bash.
checks.fix: Replace the runner allow with specific inner-command rules for exactly the tasks you need.

checks.title: WebFetch is allowed for all domains
checks.detect: permissions.allow contains a bare `WebFetch` rule in {file}
checks.why: A bare WebFetch allow lets Claude fetch any URL, which can pull in prompt-injection content or be used to phone data out via the URL.
checks.fix: Restrict to specific domains: `WebFetch(domain:docs.anthropic.com)`, etc.

checks.title: WebFetch preflight safety check is disabled
checks.detect: settings has `skipWebFetchPreflight: true` in {file}
checks.why: Turning off the preflight check means you lose Anthropic's malicious-domain blocklist on fetched URLs.
checks.fix: Remove "skipWebFetchPreflight": true (or set it to false), and pair WebFetch with a domain allowlist.

checks.title: MCP tools are broadly auto-allowed
checks.detect: permissions.allow contains a broad `mcp__*` rule, or write-capable MCP tools are auto-allowed, in {file}
checks.why: A broad mcp__ allow auto-approves every tool on every connected MCP server. Anthropic does not security-audit third-party MCP servers, so this can hand wide capability to code you didn't vet.
checks.fix: Scope MCP permissions per server and per tool, e.g. `mcp__github__list_issues`, instead of a blanket mcp__ rule.

checks.title: Write-capable MCP connectors are configured
checks.detect: `.mcp.json` declares connectors that can send email, move money, or delete data ({value})
checks.why: Connectors that can take real-world actions (send email, transfer funds, delete records) deserve a deliberate trust decision — a prompt injection that reaches them can act as you.
checks.fix: Confirm you intend each connector. Apply least privilege: scope tokens/scopes down, and only allow the specific tools you use.

checks.title: A hook runs automatically — here's what it does
checks.detect: a hook runs a command at some event (e.g. SessionStart, PreToolUse), declared in {file}; the command itself is shown in the evidence
checks.why: A hook is a command that runs on its own when something happens — like when Claude starts up or uses a tool — without asking each time. This one is written right in your settings, so it's not hidden: you can see exactly what it runs (shown below). Most hooks are things you set up yourself, like loading a notes file at startup. Just worth a glance to be sure it's yours.
checks.fix: Read the command shown. If you recognise it or set it up, you're all set. If you don't recognise it, remove it or check what it does.

checks.title: Additional working directories are too broad
checks.detect: settings `additionalDirectories` (or `--add-dir`) includes a broad path like ~, /, or a home root in {file}
checks.why: additionalDirectories widens what Claude can reach beyond the project. A broad entry like your home folder or filesystem root massively enlarges the blast radius.
checks.fix: Narrow additionalDirectories to the specific extra folders you actually need.

checks.title: No Read deny rules protecting secrets
checks.detect: no permissions.deny Read rule covers .env / secrets / ssh keys across {file}
checks.why: Without a Read deny rule, nothing stops Claude from opening files like .env, *.pem, or ~/.ssh directly.
checks.fix: Add Read deny rules: { "permissions": { "deny": ["Read(./.env)", "Read(**/.env)", "Read(**/.env.*)", "Read(**/*.pem)", "Read(**/id_rsa)", "Read(~/.ssh/**)", "Read(./secrets/**)"] } }
checks.caveatContextual: A deny rule stops me from opening the file directly, but a script you or I run in this project could still read it. That matters most when there's actually a live secret there — otherwise it's just worth knowing for later.

checks.title: OS-level sandbox is not enabled
checks.detect: no sandbox configuration detected in settings ({file})
checks.why: A deny rule is a guardrail inside Claude Code; the sandbox is an OS-level boundary that even a script we run can't step outside of. Without it, the airtight protection isn't on.
checks.fix: Consider enabling the sandbox via /sandbox for OS-level isolation.
checks.caveatContextual: Worth turning on now if the scan found a real exposure (it's the airtight fix); otherwise just good to know for when you start handling sensitive data.

checks.title: Consumer plan may use your data for training
checks.detect: [judgment] plan appears to be Free/Pro/Max (not detectable from local files)
checks.why: On consumer plans, your data — including code — may be used to train models, with up to 5-year retention. This is a posture note, not a vulnerability.
checks.fix: Review your data controls at claude.ai/settings/data-privacy-controls. This check is informational; the scanner cannot read your plan from local files.

checks.title: Conversation transcripts are stored locally
checks.detect: plaintext transcripts live under ~/.claude/projects/ (default retention {value} days)
checks.why: Claude Code keeps local plaintext transcripts of your sessions (default 30 days). For sensitive work these can linger on disk.
checks.fix: Set "cleanupPeriodDays" in settings to a shorter window for sensitive work, e.g. { "cleanupPeriodDays": 7 }.

checks.title: Telemetry / reporting state
checks.detect: state of DISABLE_TELEMETRY / DISABLE_ERROR_REPORTING / DISABLE_FEEDBACK_COMMAND / CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC in settings env ({value})
checks.why: Telemetry and error reporting send usage data (not your code or file paths). The /feedback command, however, does include code. This is a posture note so you can choose your comfort level.
checks.fix: To opt out, set these in settings env: { "env": { "DISABLE_TELEMETRY": "1", "DISABLE_ERROR_REPORTING": "1", "CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC": "1" } }.

checks.title: No CLAUDE.md found
checks.detect: no CLAUDE.md (or AGENTS.md) in the project root
checks.why: Without a CLAUDE.md, Claude has no project-specific guidance — conventions, what's off-limits, how to route work — so it guesses more and you repeat yourself.
checks.fix: Generate a right-sized CLAUDE.md (the /fantasia-safety-setup interview can do this for you).

checks.title: CLAUDE.md is oversized
checks.detect: a CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md exceeds the size guideline ({value})
checks.why: Oversized always-loaded context wastes tokens and, past a point, makes the model 'get confused' rather than better-informed.
checks.fix: Trim CLAUDE.md to the essentials; move rarely-needed detail into referenced files loaded on demand.

checks.title: Conflicting / duplicated context files
checks.detect: multiple nested CLAUDE.md files, or both CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md present ({value})
checks.why: Conflicting nested CLAUDE.md files, or a CLAUDE.md that duplicates AGENTS.md, can give the model contradictory instructions.
checks.fix: Reconcile the files: keep one source of truth per scope and remove duplication.
